beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Hola, Liam — in for Bear. That belief has a testable consequence — a number that cannot exceed 2? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: That belief has a testable consequence — a number that cannot exceed 2?

beats.narration_text: Einstein believed particles carry hidden pre-set answers before measurement. That belief has a testable consequence — a number that cannot exceed 2.

card.headline: Why 2 Becomes 2√2 — How Bell Beat Einstein

beats.narration_text: Alice and Bob each choose between two spin-measurement axes. The CHSH score combines four correlations. Any world with pre-set answers: score at most 2. That is the classical ceiling.
beats.description: Two detector icons (Alice, Bob) each with two axis choices. Equation: |E(a₁,b₁)+E(a₁,b₂)+E(a₂,b₁)−E(a₂,b₂)| ≤ 2. CRIMSON border around the bound '2'.

beats.narration_text: The question: if every experiment produces a score above 2, what does that mean for Einstein's picture?

card.headline: Can experiment push the score above 2?

beats.narration_text: For the singlet — two entangled spin-half particles — the quantum correlation between axes at angle θ is minus cosine θ. Choose the four axes at 0°, 45°, 90°, 135° and compute.
beats.description: Four angle labels: a₁=0°, b₁=45°, a₂=90°, b₂=135°. Correlation formula: E_QM = −cosθ in TEAL. Each pairwise angle difference labeled.

beats.narration_text: Each correlation is minus one over root 2. Combine four of them: the CHSH score is 2 root 2 — about 2.83. That is 41 percent above the classical ceiling.
beats.description: Step: each E = −1/√2 ≈ −0.707. CHSH sum = −4/√2 = −2√2. |S| = 2√2 ≈ 2.828. TEAL highlighted. Classical 2 shown below in CRIMSON. Gap labeled '41%'.

beats.narration_text: A classical correlated pair gives linear correlations. The singlet's quantum correlation follows minus cosine θ — a curve, not a line. The curve is more extreme near 45°, and that is exactly where CHSH probes it.
beats.description: Plot: x-axis angle θ from 0° to 180°. Classical (linear, CRIMSON dashed) vs quantum (−cosθ curve, TEAL solid). At θ=45° gap is visible. CHSH probing angles marked with teal dots.

beats.narration_text: Aspect measured 2.697 in 1982. Loophole-free tests in 2015 confirmed 2.828 at ten standard deviations. Local hidden variables are ruled out, not just unlikely.
beats.description: Number line: 0 to 3. Marker at 2 (CRIMSON, 'LHV max'). Marker at 2√2≈2.83 (TEAL, 'Tsirelson bound / quantum max'). Arrow at 2.697 labeled 'Aspect 1982'. Arrow at 2.828 labeled '2015 loophole-free'.

beats.narration_text: The Tsirelson bound shows 2 root 2 is the highest any quantum state can score — no quantum trick can go further. Between 2 and 2 root 2 sits everything quantum mechanics allows but local hidden variables forbid.

props.headline: The Tsirelson bound shows 2 root 2 is the highest any…

beats.still_prompt: B10, clean educational diagram: a horizontal number line from 0 to 3. At value 2: a red vertical bar labeled 'Classical ceiling (LHV ≤ 2)'. At value 2.828: a teal vertical bar labeled 'Tsirelson / Quantum max (2√2)'. The region between 2 and 2.828 is shaded teal and labeled 'Quantum territory'. To the left of 2 is shaded crimson and labeled 'Classical territory'. Black background, white axis and text labels, clean physics illustration.

beats.narration_text: Entanglement does not allow faster-than-light signaling. Bob's marginal outcome is random regardless of Alice's choice. To see the correlation, you must compare data — and that comparison travels at light speed.
beats.description: Alice and Bob separated by large distance. Alice flips a coin choosing axis. Bob's outcome histogram: same random 50/50 either way. Labels: 'No FTL signal — marginal is random'. Only after classical comparison: correlation appears.

beats.narration_text: Singlet: E = −cosθ. Angles: a₁=0°, b₁=45°, a₂=90°, b₂=135°. E₁₁ = −cos 45° = −1/√2. E₁₂ = −cos 45° = −1/√2. E₂₁ = −cos 45° = −1/√2. E₂₂ = −cos 135° = +1/√2. CHSH sum: 4 times 1/√2 equals 2√2.

Can experiment push the score above 2? Yes — to exactly 2 root 2. That 41-percent gap is the experimental signature of quantum entanglement, ruling out every local pre-set-answer theory Einstein imagined.

card.headline:
Entanglement scores 2√2.
Pre-set answers are dead.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Singlet: E = −cosθ. Angles: a₁=0°, b₁=45°, a₂=90°, b₂=135°. E₁₁ = −cos 45° = −1/√2. E₁₂ = −cos 45° = −1/√2. E₂₁ = −cos 45° = −1/√2. E₂₂ = −cos 135° = +1/√2. CHSH sum: 4 times 1/√2 equals 2√2. Can experiment push the score above 2? Yes — to exactly 2 root 2. That 41-percent gap is the experimental signature of quantum entanglement, ruling out every local pre-set-answer theory Einstein imagined.

artifactLines: Can experiment push the score above 2?

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [the mechanism just described] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?

props.title: vox-chsh-ceiling
props.subline: that 41-percent gap is the experimental signature of quantum entanglement, rulin
